coredata.dc:title: AgPt hollow nanodendrites based on N doping graphene quantum dots for enhanced methanol electrooxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Tao
Zhang, Qiankun
Li, Jinling
He, Shijie
Zhang, Dongxia
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) require high-performance electrocatalysts for utilization at the commercial scale. In this paper, a novel, high-efficiency electrocatalyst composed of silver-platinum hollow nanodendrites based on N doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs), which were synthesized via an easy wet chemical method, for the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is demonstrated. The photomediated Ag/N-GQDs nanoparticles (Ag/N-GQDs NPs) were firstly prepared under the condition of visible light illumination (the central wavelength is 589 nm), and then galvanic replacement reaction occurred between PtCl6 2- and Ag nanoparticles to obtain the final product. The morphology of the catalyst exhibits a uniform hollow structure by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX). The effects of the different mole ratio of Ag/Pt on the catalytic activity have been studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA). Under the optimal conditions, N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs show the excellent electrocatalytic activity of 2207.6 mA mgPt −1 towards MOR, which is about 21-fold higher than that of the commercial Pt/C. Interestingly, the photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs also caused the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts to display a 1.7-fold enhancement in catalytic activity compared to that of the untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs. Moreover, the CO tolerance and stability of the catalysts for MOR are also greatly improved. The present approach would provide an environment-friendly and valuable way to prepare multi-component catalysts with hollow nanostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
AgPt alloy
Hollow nanodendrites
Methanol oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs with the different ratio
Characterization of the photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs
Electrochemical performance of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs electrocatalyst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China , China ( 21165016 , 21175108 , and 21265018 ), the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province , China (No.s 1011GKCA025 , 090GKCA036 , 1208RJZM289 ), and the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University , China ( 2020KYZZ001163 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of N-GQDs (a), untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs (b), and photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs (c); inset: size distribution of N-GQDs, untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs, and photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs, respectively. Histogram of the zeta potential (d) of untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs (1) and photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs (2). HRTEM image (e) of single photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs. SAED patterns (f) of photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs.
TEM image (a), SAED image (b), HRTEM image (c), and HAADF-STEM image (d) of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs.
HAADF-STEM elemental mapping images (a) of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs. Elemental line scanning over the entire particle region indicated by red-colored rectangle area in inset of b.
Survey (a), high-resolution C 1s (b), N 1s (c), O 1s (d), Ag 3d (e), and Pt 4f (f) XPS spectra of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs.
The formation mechanism of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs at different stages: (a) incipient stage; (b) reaction stage.

CV curves for different Ag/Pt mole ratios of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts in 1.0 M NaOH solution + 0.5 M methanol (a) and in 1.0 M NaOH solution (b); CA curves for different Ag/Pt mole ratios of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts in 1.0 M NaOH solution + 0.5 M methanol (c); EIS Nyquist plots for different Ag/Pt mole ratios of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts in 0.1 M K3[Fe(CN)6] + 0.1 K4[Fe(CN)6] + 1.0 M KCl solution (d).

CV curves of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (with Ag/Pt = 0.8) and Pt/C catalyst-modifited electrodes in 1.0 M NaOH + 0.5 M CH3OH solution (a) and 1.0 M NaOH solution (b); CA curves for N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (with Ag/Pt = 0.8) and Pt/C catalyst-modifited electrodes in 1.0 M NaOH solution + 0.5 M methanol (c); EIS Nyquist plots for N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (with Ag/Pt = 0.8) and Pt/C catalyst-modifited electrodes in 0.1 M K3[Fe(CN)6] + 0.1 K4[Fe(CN)6] + 1.0 M KCl solution (d).
The synthesis process of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs.

head.ce:title: AgPt hollow nanodendrites based on N doping graphene quantum dots for enhanced methanol electrooxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) require high-performance electrocatalysts for utilization at the commercial scale. In this paper, a novel, high-efficiency electrocatalyst composed of silver-platinum hollow nanodendrites based on N doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs), which were synthesized via an easy wet chemical method, for the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is demonstrated. The photomediated Ag/N-GQDs nanoparticles (Ag/N-GQDs NPs) were firstly prepared under the condition of visible light illumination (the central wavelength is 589 nm), and then galvanic replacement reaction occurred between PtCl6 2- and Ag nanoparticles to obtain the final product. The morphology of the catalyst exhibits a uniform hollow structure by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX). The effects of the different mole ratio of Ag/Pt on the catalytic activity have been studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA). Under the optimal conditions, N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs show the excellent electrocatalytic activity of 2207.6 mA mgPt −1 towards MOR, which is about 21-fold higher than that of the commercial Pt/C. Interestingly, the photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs also caused the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts to display a 1.7-fold enhancement in catalytic activity compared to that of the untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs. Moreover, the CO tolerance and stability of the catalysts for MOR are also greatly improved. The present approach would provide an environment-friendly and valuable way to prepare multi-component catalysts with hollow nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have been studied extensively owing to the high theoretical energy conversion efficiency (97%) and easy fuel storage [1–5]. Up to now, it has been proved that Pt-based nanoparticles are the most effective anode catalysts for DMFCs. However, CO species yielding in the course of methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) would poison the surface of Pt via occupying catalytically active sites, resulting in a severe decrease in the efficiency of catalytic activity [6–8]. In addition, the high cost and large consumption of pure Pt catalysts seriously hinder the commercialization of DMFCs [9]. Therefore, a lot of efforts have been dedicated to enhancing the utilization efficiency of Pt in the field of DMFCs.

Alloying Pt metal with other metals (M) such as Ru [10], Ni [11], Au [12], Co [13], and Ag [14]is an efficient method for improving the CO tolerance and catalytic activity of Pt-based catalysts, which can be explained with the bifunctional mechanism. When combined with Pt, H2O and OH are bound preferentially to M atoms. And adsorbed OH on M sites promotes oxidative removal of COads from the surface of Pt atoms through the reaction: Pt-CO + M-OH → Pt-COOH + M [15]. Ag has been identified to facilitate the formation of AgPt alloy due to its similar lattice constants to Pt 16,17]. Besides, introduction of Ag can modify the electronic structure and promote the activation of surface active sites [18,19]. The Ag nanoparticles, on the other hand, exhibit increased photochemical activity due to their high specific surface area and unusual electronic properties [20]. In terms of light-induced transformation, visible light can change the morphology of Ag nanoparticles because of a strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect, which can considerably increase the efficient photoexcited electron-hole separation and the charge carrier transfer properties at the Ag-metal oxides interface [21]. Hence, basic understanding of the morphological changes under light irradiation are also significant for developing a simple method to optimize the performance of Ag nanoparticles.

Moreover, the support materials including carbon nanosheets [22], porous carbon [23], graphene [24], and carbon quantum dots [25] have been widely employed to prevent aggregation of noble metal nanomaterials. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as an advisable carrier for integration with various metal nanoparticles, because of their particularly high conductivity and specific surface area with the existence of various functional groups [26]. Meanwhile, the GQDs electronic density, optical, and electrical properties can be changed via doping other elements (Nitrogen, Sulfur, or Phosphorous elements, etc.) [27,28]. The N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), having an effective tuning of electronic, excellent assist in dispersing the metal nanoparticles, and optical properties along with localized chemical features, have widely applied for photoelectrocatalysis [29–31]. In addition, the N-GQDs were also found to be favorable for providing a high number of anchoring sites for the adsorption of metal ions [32], as well as supplying more active sites for the electrooxidation of MOR [33].

The morphologies and construction of the catalysts have a significant influence that controls their performance and stability. Compared with solid nanoparticles, the hollow nanodendrites significantly enlarge the active surface areas because they possess exterior and interior surfaces [34–36]. Moreover, hollow nanostructures can effectively reduce the nanomaterial density, so that they can be applied in a lot of aspects, such as biomedical, light-weight devices, surface-enhanced Raman and electrochemical catalysis, etc. [37,38]. At present, hollow nanostructures were usually synthesized via the template method [39], the Kirkendall effect [40], and the galvanic replacement reaction [41]. Especially, the galvanic replacement, utilizing the different reduction potentials between two metals, is a simple and cost-effectively means to synthesis hollow metallic nanostructures. Thus, it is significant to design and fabricate a hollow Pt-based nanocatalyst which can efficiently utilize Pt [42].

Herein, we utilized the N-GQDs as reducing agents and dispersing agents for Ag nanoparticles, through a simple visible light illumination treatment to prepare photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs. Following, we used the galvanic replacement reaction to synthesize the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs at room temperature, with the AgPt alloy on inside and outside surfaces of the N-GQDs. The detailed synthesis procedures were depicted in Scheme 1. Furthermore, catalytic properties have been measured by monitoring the electrical current density changes for MOR. The results suggest that catalytic properties and stability of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs are better than commercial Pt/C.

Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6.6H2O), Silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid, urea, ascorbic acid and other reagents used in this work were all of analytical grade and the secondary distilled water used in the whole experiments.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI TECNAI20, USA) images of these nanomaterials were applied to analyze the structural properties and morphological. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was probed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Zeta potentials were measured through a Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN 3600. Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured by a Cary 500 Scan UV–vis-near-infrared spectrometer.

The synthesis of N-GQDs was based on the previous report [43], 2.1 g citric acid and 1.8 g urea were mixed into 50 mL secondary distilled water at 25 °C under stirring for 3 h. And then the solution was poured into 80 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave, which was then heated in an oven at 160 °C for 480 min. After cooling down 25 °C, the solution was dialyzed for 2 days under a 3500 Da dialysis bag.

Ag/N-GQDs NPs were synthesized via redox reaction between N-GQDs and AgNO3, 250 μL N-GQDs were added into 50 mL round-bottom flask under stirring for 2 min. Then, 200 μL AgNO3 (10 mM) was put into the above solution, which was heated at 100 °C for 30 min under reflux. The preparation was aged at 4 °C for 12 h, and then illuminated at 70 W high-pressure sodium lamp (HPLS) for 180 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs with the different ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs were synthesized by replacement reaction and reduction. 400 μL AA (0.1 M) was added into photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs solution (10 mL) with stirring for 2 min. Then, 125 μL H2PtCl6.6H2O (19.6 mM) was put into the above solution with fast stirring for 30 min at room temperature. The products were extracted by centrifuging at 13,000 rpm for 10 min, and then repeated washings with alcohol and secondary distilled water (1:1). For comparison, the Ag/Pt = 1.2, Ag/Pt = 1.0 and Ag/Pt = 0.6 samples were also prepared by varying the feed ratio of AgNO3 and H2PtCl6.

All electrochemical tests were detected using a three-electrode system ( a Pt wire as the counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 3 mm) as the working electrode), which were based on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China). 2 mg different ratio samples of Ag/Pt were dispersed in 2 mL secondary distilled water, respectively. Then, 5 μL of above suspension liquid (1 mg/mL) was dropped onto a cleanly GCE and dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The obtained N-GQDs with an average diameter of 2.67 ± 0.7 nm show a uniform morphology in TEM image ( Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b and c showed the TEM images of Ag/N-GQDs NPs under dark treatment and visible light illumination treatment, respectively. As clearly seen, compared with untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs, the photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs are more uniform, which are nearly similar to the spherical with an average size of 16 ± 2.1 nm. The reason is that the small Ag/N-GQDs NPs were oxidized by the dissolved oxygen under illumination (Eq. (1)) due to its higher surface energy [44].
(1) Ag0 + 1/2O2 + H2O → Ag+ + 2OH─

Fig. 1d showed the zeta potential of untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs was measured to be − 46.3 mV (column 1). After visible light illumination for 180 min, the amount of the surface negative charge of photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs decline to 43.0 (column 2). This change could be explained by electrostatic adsorption between Ag/N-GQDs NPs and Ag+ ions, which indicates that the Ag+ adhere to the surface of the Ag/N-GQDs NPs [45]. Furthermore, as the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1e) shown that the lattice fringes of the photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs are very clear, ordered, and continuous, with a lattice spacing of 0.235 nm and 0.238 nm, which is correspond to the {111} planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) Ag and the {100} planes of fcc graphite, respectively. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs shows several bright consecutive concentric rings (Fig. 1f), which corresponding to fcc Ag {111}, Ag {220}, Ag {311} and graphite {100}, respectively.

As seen in Fig. 2a and b, the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs with an average of 22.2 ± 3.1 nm are relatively uniform hollow dendrite structure and polycrystalline. Moreover, control experiments were carried out to verify the influence of illumination on the final structures. Fig. S1 shows the untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs yield a smoother shell of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs. This phenomenon can be explained as the Ag+ ions on the surface of photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs were reduced to Ag atoms which induce more dendrite formation. These observations demonstrate that the illumination is beneficial to improve the catalytic performance of the electrocatalysts. From the HRTEM image (Fig. 2c), it is evident that the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs possessed dendrites, which are quite favorable for increase the active site. The lattice fringes at the edge of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs with a lattice spacing of 0.230 nm, 0.229 nm, and 0.234 nm, which are all assigned to the AgPt alloy {111} crystal planes [46], implying the synthesis of AgPt alloy. Furthermore, high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) image of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (Fig. 2d) displays an obvious hollow feature by the contrast sharply between the shells and the central position. Meanwhile, the HADDF-STEM elemental mapping images ( Fig. 3a) also demonstrate the formation of AgPt alloy. The EDX elemental line scanning profiles (Fig. 3b) more explicitly shows that the coexistence of Ag and Pt elements across all of the crystal, and the contents of Ag and Pt were mainly existed in the boundary layer, further prove the synthesis of AgPt alloy and hollow structure.

The chemical state and compositions of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs were investigated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [35,47]. As illustrated by the survey XPS spectrum ( Fig. 4a), the catalysts include C, O, N, Ag, and Pt elements. The high-resolution XPS of C 1s (Fig. 4b) can be reasonably consistent with C═C (284 eV), C─C (286 eV), and O─C═O (289 eV) carbon functional groups [48]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) shows two peaks at 401 eV and 397 eV, which are corresponded to the N─H (or graphitic N) and C─N─C (pyrrolic N), respectively [43]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the O 1s spectrum was divided into two peaks which are attribute to C═O (532 eV) and C─O/C─O─C (533 eV) [49]. Fig. 4e was displayed the representative XPS spectra of Ag 3d, which is only a pair of peaks that appeared at 367.5 eV and 373.5 eV, corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 of metallic Ag0, indicating the efficient reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 atoms [50]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Pt 4f (Fig. 4f) could be deconstructed into two pairs of peaks. The stronger pair at 70.94 eV and 74.3 eV were corresponded to Pt0, and the weaker couples at 72.1 eV and 75.4 eV ascribed to Pt2+ [47]. Moreover, the peak of Pt 4f and Ag 3d shift to higher and lower binding energy relative to the pure Pt (70.90 eV and 74.25 eV) and pure Ag (367.90 eV and 373.80 eV), respectively. The shift of the binding energies demonstrates that the electron transfer from Pt to Ag, which is related to the electronic interaction between Pt and Ag atomic orbitals due to the formation of AgPt alloy [51,52].

In this instance, the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs were synthesized at the precursor of Ag/Pt mole ratio of 0.8. For the typical synthesis, the N-GQDs might function as both dispersing agents and carriers for the growth of AgPt alloy because of the numerous active sites, which are come from various oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. At the incipient stage, the Ag+ ions preferentially formed on the surface of photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs due to illuminating with visible light ( Fig. 5a). Then, the following actions were most probably happening after adding AA and H2PtCl6.6H2O. (i) The Ag+ ions on the surface of Ag/N-GQDs NPs were reduced to Ag by AA, which was attached to the external surface of N-GQDs. (ii) Fig. 5b described when PtCl6 2- ions approached the Ag core of Ag/N-GQDs NPs, the galvanic replacement reaction occurred (Eq. (2)) [41]. Ag starts decomposing into Ag+ inside the N-GQDs shell, followed the Ag+ ions were transferred to outside the N-GQDs shell via diffusing, where there are plenty of PtCl6 2- ions present, and then both metal ions get reduced by AA to produce AgPt on the outer surface of the N-GQDs shell.
(2) 4Ag + PtCl6 2- → Pt + 4Ag+ + 6Cl─

(iii) AA from the bulk solution diffused to inside through the N-GQDs shell, where there are a lot of dissolved Ag+ ions and Pt, the Ag+ ions get reduced by AA to Ag. Then, the newly-formed Ag and Pt atoms continually fused together to shape AgPt alloy spherical particles inside the N-GQDs shell [53].

Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs, photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs, and N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs shown in Fig. S2. Fig. S2(a) displays a typical absorption peak at 230 nm and 335 nm, which were designated to π-π* and n-π* transition of graphitic sp2 domains [54], respectively. The photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs cleanly showed an absorption peak located at 400 nm, which was ascribed to the SPR of Ag NPs (Fig. S2(b)). As seen in Fig. S2(c), with the increasing of AgPt alloy was synthesized, the absorption peak was gradually suppressed [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs electrocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performance of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs towards the MOR was tested by CV in 4 mL of 1 M NaOH + 0.5 M methanol solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. For ease of comparison, the electrocatalytic performance of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs for MOR was tested using CV, CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The mass activities of different Ag/Pt mole ratios of the electrocatalysts have also been summarized in Fig. 6a, the peak current density with the Ag/Pt mole ratio of 0.8 (2207.6 mA mgPt −1) was about 1.5-fold higher than the Ag/Pt mole ratio of 1.2 (1449.5 mA mgPt −1), 1.2-fold higher than Ag/Pt ratio of 1.0 (1784.2 mA mgPt −1), and 1.7-fold higher than Ag/Pt ratio of 0.6 (1301.1 mA mgPt −1). Meanwhile, with the amount increases of Pt that the onset potential on the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts modified electrode is more negative, which suggests that the methanol can be easier oxidized. Moreover, the catalytic properties of the four types of Ag/N-GQDs NPs under visible light irradiation and dark condition have been outlined in Fig. S4, as shown, all of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs electrocatalysts, which were synthesised through photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs, achieved about 1.7-fold enhancement of catalytic activity than that of they were synthesised through untreated Ag/N-GQDs NPs. It’s attribute to the more dispersed, unified, and abundance dendritic N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs were synthesized via photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs is beneficial to MOR. What’s more, the Ag/N-GQDs NPs absorb the visible light, and then the electron is excited to the excited state. This process facilitates the formation of active oxygen (·O2) and hydroxyl (·OH) radical, which can facilitate methanol oxidation [43,56,57].

The Electrochemical Active Surface Area (ECSA) of the catalysts could be calculated by the following Eq. (3) [58].
(3)
E C S A =
Q
H
0.21 ×
M
P t
Where QH (in mC cm−2) is the charge transfer for hydrogen adsorption, 0.21 mC−2 is the charge for monolayer hydrogen adsorption on Pt surface and MPt is the amount of Pt loaded on the GC electrode. As seen in Fig. 6b, the CV of different Ag/Pt mole ratios of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts in 1 M NaOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The current peaks between −1.0 and −0.2 were ascribed to hydrogen adsorption/desorption at the Pt catalysts [59]. The ECSA of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (Ag/Pt = 0.8) was calculated to be 25.0 m2g−1, which is above those calculated for Ag/Pt = 1.2 (15.3 m2g−1), Ag/Pt = 1.0 (14.2 m2g−1) and Ag/Pt = 0.6 (8.1 m2g−1) samples. Moreover, the large ECSA of the hydrogen adsorption/desorption peaks of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts indicates better use of Pt for these nanostructures.

The durability is another factor for evaluating this catalyst’s electrocatalytic properties, which is important for their practical application. To research the stability of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts for MOR, the CA measurement was conducted in 1.0 M NaOH + 0.5 M CH3OH solution for 500 s at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 6c, all of the CA curves of the electrocatalysts were quickly decreased at the beginning stage, which is ascribed to the catalysts poisoning via the accumulation of intermediate products such as COads on the Pt active sites [60]. After 500 s, all of the currents of the electrocatalysts were trended to steady. In addition, the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts prepared at Ag/Pt mole ratios of 0.8 display the best property of stability. Therefore, suitable Ag/Pt mole ratios can improve electrocatalytic stability in methanol oxidation. Finally, to verify the structure of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs have transformed after the CA test. The TEM image (Fig. S3) of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs after the 500 s CA test were characterized. It is found that although the catalysts were agglomerated slightly, the hollow dendrite structure can still be clearly seen.

To fully observe the specific interfacial process and kinetic, the EIS was conducted for N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts. As it can be observed in Fig. 6d, when the Ag/Pt mole ratio of 0.8 that the diameter of the impedance arc (DIA) is smallest, which indicates the smallest charge-transfer resistance and the best electrical conductivity for the MOR [61].

In conclusion, the catalytic activity was the best that when the Ag/Pt mole ratio of 0.8, so this ratio was chosen as a comparison with commercial Pt/C. As is shown in Fig. 7a, the peak current density of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (Ag/Pt = 0.8) catalysts electrode (2207.6 mA mgPt −1) is above 21-fold that of the commercial Pt/C catalysts electrode (105.2 mA mgPt −1). In addition, the ECSA values (Fig. 7b) of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (Ag/Pt = 0.8) catalysts and commercial Pt/C were estimated to be 25.0 m2g−1, and 2.8 m2g−1, respectively. The hollow structure and AgPt alloy which attaches to the exterior and interior surfaces of N-GQDs can be capitalized on catalysis, resulting in a large ECSA. Then, as displayed in Fig. 7c, the current density rate of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (Ag/Pt = 0.8) electrode was slower than that of the commercial Pt/C electrode, which indicates the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts exhibit excellent catalytic stability. The EIS was compared in Fig. 7d, the charge-transfer resistance of the N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs (Ag/Pt = 0.8) electrode was lower than that of the commercial Pt/C electrode, which suggests faster reaction kinetics of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs catalysts for MOR. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S5, exhibiting the catalytic activity and stability of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs have better than the Hollow AgPt, due to the N-GQDs provide multidirectional electron transfer route between AgPt alloy and menthanol [31,62].

The high catalytic activity combined with the excellent stability of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs can be based on the following reasons: (i) The synergistic and electronic effects evolved from Pt, Ag, and N also be conductive to boosting the change transfer and resulting in the enhancement of performances [18]. (ii) The Ag+ ions on the surface of photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs lead to generating more dendrite structures for N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs, so that the specific surface area increases of these catalysts. (iii) According to the bifunctional mechanism, the Ag can activate water to produce more-OH or other oxygen-containing species to oxidize CO like intermediates on N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs surface [63,64]. (iv) This characteristic of hollow nanodendrites provides a possibility to integrate the function of internal and external surface of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs into synergetic collective properties, which provide more active reaction sites for MOR.

In summary, we have demonstrated the syntheses of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs via a simple wet chemical reduction and visible light illumination method. Due to the synergistic effect and high surface active areas evolved from hollow dendritic nanostructures, the resultant N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs showed 21-fold improvements in catalytic activity than that of commercial Pt/C for MOR. Amazingly, because of the surface atoms dissociation of Ag/N-GQDs NPs by visible light illumination, the electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/AgPt HNDs synthesized via photomediated Ag/N-GQDs NPs towards MOR showed 1.7-fold higher than that without visible light treatment. Given the easy design and the excellent catalytic performance of these nanomaterials, there seems to show great potential for the application of DMFCs.

Tao Shao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Qiankun Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Jinling Li: Writing - review & editing. Shijie He: Writing - review & editing. Dongxia Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Xibin Zhou: Writing - review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (21165016, 21175108, and 21265018), the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province, China (No.s 1011GKCA025, 090GKCA036, 1208RJZM289), and the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University, China (2020KYZZ001163).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.160607.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
